questions.question: List and briefly describe the two core components of a block header that are critical for maintaining the chronological order and immutability of the blockchain.
keyPoints: Previous Block Hash: A cryptographic hash of the previous block's header, linking the blocks together and ensuring the chain's integrity.
Timestamp: A record of the approximate time the block was created, helping to establish a chronological order of events on the chain.

questions.question: Describe the purpose and function of the Merkle Root within a block header. Explain how it contributes to the security and efficiency of the blockchain.
keyPoints: The Merkle Root is a single cryptographic hash that represents all the transactions in the block.
It enables efficient and secure verification of whether a specific transaction is included in the block without downloading all transactions (using Merkle Proofs).
It ensures data integrity; any change to a single transaction would invalidate the Merkle Root and consequently the block's hash, making tampering evident.

questions.question: Explain the role of the Nonce and the Difficulty Target in the block header within the Proof-of-Work consensus mechanism. Detail the process of mining and how a valid block hash is found.
keyPoints: The Difficulty Target is a value that defines how difficult it is to find a valid hash for the new block, ensuring a consistent block creation time.
The Nonce is a variable that miners repeatedly change in their search for a block hash that is numerically lower than the Difficulty Target.

Mining is the process of hashing the block header with trillions of different nonce values until one produces a valid hash that meets the target. This requires significant computational power, securing the network against attack.
